ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for chicken katsu cheese sauce curry rice; closest defensible anchor is 'curry rice food product' (FOODON_00002809). Cheese sauce and chicken cutlet components are not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Curry rice) is the closest defensible match. The cheese sauce and breaded chicken cutlet toppings are not separately represented in FoodEx2 at this level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for chicken katsu cheese sauce curry rice. SNOMED CT food coding is not granular enough for this composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of chicken cutlet cheese sauce curry rice is approximately 400 g, comprising a mound of steamed rice topped with Japanese curry sauce, a breaded fried chicken cutlet, and a drizzle of cheese sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Chicken Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice (cr_id G00010938). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (breaded fried chicken + curry rice + cheese sauce) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White rice and curry rice analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken katsu cheese sauce curry rice. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese curry rice (GI ~65, medium category). The dominant carbohydrate source is steamed white rice with curry sauce; the breaded chicken cutlet and cheese sauce contribute fat and protein that further moderate glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × net carbohydrate per serving (64 g, accounting for fibre offset from 100.76 g total carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 41.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; partially offset by high potassium 327 mg/100 g (Na:K ratio < 1, favourable for blood pressure)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g per meal to keep sodium intake below 500 mg per serving, and request reduced-sodium curry roux and a smaller cheese sauce portion; the high potassium content (327 mg/100 g) provides a DASH-diet-compatible offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g; estimated GI 65 (medium); high glycaemic load per standard 400 g plate (~41.6 GL units)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit the serving to 200–250 g and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce glycaemic load; the 3.7 g/100 g dietary fibre moderates postprandial glucose rise, but the large standard portion size and medium GI make portion control essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~1.8 g/100 g from deep-fried breaded chicken and cheese sauce; sodium 231 mg/100 g; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100 g in HPB source (likely underreported for a fried chicken dish)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a pan-fried or baked chicken cutlet to reduce saturated fat and AGE formation, choose a smaller cheese sauce portion, and limit the serving to 250 g to keep saturated fat and sodium within heart-healthy daily targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; curry spices (turmeric, ginger) provide anti-inflammatory polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request a pan-fried or baked chicken cutlet to minimise AGE formation, and may benefit from the anti-inflammatory polyphenols in curry spices; those with nightshade sensitivity should note that potato is a common curry ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice is a Japanese-style fusion rice dish popular in Singapore's Japanese casual-dining and food-court restaurants. The dish consists of a generous mound of steamed Japanese short-grain or medium-grain white rice topped with a rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce (typically made from a commercial curry roux block with onion, potato, and carrot), a panko-breadcrumb-coated deep-fried chicken cutlet (chicken katsu), and a creamy cheese sauce drizzled over the top. The result is a hearty, umami-rich plate combining the crispy texture of the fried chicken, the soft stickiness of the rice, the warming sweetness of Japanese curry, and the savoury richness of melted cheese. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g and potassium is notably high at 327 mg/100 g. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten, dairy, or egg allergies. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner meal in Singapore's Japanese restaurant chains and hawker-adjacent food courts. Known in Japanese as チキンカツチーズソースカレーライス (Chikin Katsu Chīzu Sōsu Karē Raisu), in Chinese as 鸡排芝士酱咖喱饭, in Malay as Nasi Kari Sos Keju Ayam Goreng, and widely recognised across Singapore's multicultural dining landscape.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010938, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for Japanese curry rice. All mandatory fields are populated including full multilingual coverage for Singapore-served Japanese cuisine.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) is an analogous estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central composite data for breaded fried chicken, Japanese curry sauce, and cheese sauce components; HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 3. Cholesterol is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g per the HPB source; this is likely an underreported value for a dish containing deep-fried breaded chicken and cheese sauce, but the HPB canonical value is preserved as reported. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for Japanese curry rice; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in approved sources. 5. The dish name in the input contained a trailing period ('Chicken Cutlet Cheese Sauce Curry Rice.'); the canonical food_name has been normalised by removing the trailing punctuation. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; the dish contains pork-derived gelatin risk in commercial curry roux blocks and cheese; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific restaurant. 7. This dish is Japanese in cuisine origin but is served in Singapore (region_tag: SG); multilingual local names and aliases have been provided for all five Singapore mandatory languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) in addition to Japanese, satisfying both the Japanese cuisine G6 requirement and the Singapore regional serving context.
